question: How do you deliver updates? Can updates be pushed remotely without customer approval? What's your vulnerability patching timeline?
title: Update Delivery Model, Customer Control, and Vulnerability Patching SLAs
bullets: Describe update mechanism (push vs. pull, channels, environments) and customer control over deployment
Clarify policies for security vs. feature updates (auto-apply vs. approval, deferral options)
Define vulnerability remediation SLAs by severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low) and zero-day process
Explain safeguards (signed updates, staged rollouts, canarying) and rollback procedures
suggested_inputs: Release management policy and update workflow diagrams
Change logs/release notes with pre-release visibility
Code signing and artifact verification documentation
Vulnerability management policy with SLAs and incident examples

good.text: Updates are delivered via a controlled mechanism (pull or staged push) with customer-configurable policies. Security patches can be auto-applied with opt-out/deferral windows; feature updates require explicit approval or scheduled deployment.

All updates are cryptographically signed and verified. Deployments use staged rollouts/canarying with health checks. Immediate rollback procedures are documented and tested, with version pinning supported.

Vulnerability SLAs: Critical (24–48 hours), High (≤7 days), Medium (≤30 days). Zero-day response includes rapid assessment, mitigation guidance, patch release, and customer notification within defined timelines.
Detailed change logs are published prior to deployment. Customers can review updates, defer within policy limits, and schedule maintenance windows. Historical logs and impact notes are available.

partial.text: Updates are generally pushed with limited configuration options. Customers may have some ability to schedule, but approval/deferral controls are not comprehensive.
We use signing or staged rollouts, but the full process (verification, canarying, rollback testing) is not clearly documented or consistently applied.
We address vulnerabilities promptly, but SLAs by severity and zero-day procedures are not clearly defined.
Release notes are provided, but not always in advance or with sufficient detail on impact and changes.

bad.text: Updates are automatically pushed, including security changes, without customer control or deferral options.
We cannot clearly explain how updates are delivered or controlled.
We do not define timelines for vulnerability patching.
We have never had a bad update.

follow_ups: If a malicious or compromised update were attempted, what controls prevent it from auto-deploying to all customers?
Provide recent change logs—can we review updates before they are applied and defer if needed?
Describe your rollback process and provide an example of a rollback incident.
Can we control update timing (maintenance windows) or defer updates? What are the limits?
What are your exact vulnerability SLAs by severity, and how do you handle zero-day disclosures and customer notifications?
